content: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) occurs when microorganisms evolve mechanisms to survive drug exposure, rendering treatments ineffective. AMR is a global public health threat causing approximately 1.27 million deaths annually directly and contributing to nearly 5 million deaths. ESKAPE pathogens (Enterococcus faecium, Staphylococcus aureus, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Acinetobacter baumannii, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Enterobacter species) are priority organisms due to multidrug resistance and healthcare impact. Resistance mechanisms include: enzyme production (beta-lactamases), target modification, efflux pumps, and reduced permeability. Drivers: antibiotic overuse in humans and animals, inadequate infection prevention, poor sanitation, and lack of new antibiotics in development pipeline.
source: WHO Global AMR Surveillance, CDC Antibiotic Resistance Threats Report

content: AMR containment strategies: (1) Antibiotic stewardship—prescribe only when needed, select narrowest spectrum, optimize dose/duration; (2) Infection prevention—hand hygiene, isolation precautions, environmental cleaning; (3) Vaccination—reduce infections requiring antibiotics; (4) Surveillance—track resistance patterns, detect emerging threats; (5) One Health approach—reduce agricultural antibiotic use, improve animal husbandry; (6) Sanitation infrastructure—clean water, wastewater treatment; (7) Research incentives—push/pull mechanisms for new antibiotics, alternatives like phage therapy. National action plans coordinate multisectoral responses with measurable targets and accountability.
source: WHO Global Action Plan on AMR, CDC Core Elements of Hospital Antibiotic Stewardship

content: AMR surveillance systems track resistance trends through: laboratory-based networks (reporting susceptibility patterns), population-based studies (colonization prevalence), and clinical outcome monitoring (treatment failure rates). Standardized methods (CLSI, EUCAST breakpoints) enable comparability. Key metrics: proportion of isolates resistant to first-line agents, incidence of carbapenem-resistant organisms, vancomycin-resistant enterococci rates. Global burden estimates combine resistance data with incidence models. Economic impact includes prolonged hospitalization, expensive second-line drugs, and productivity losses. Surveillance gaps exist in low-resource settings where AMR burden may be highest but detection capacity limited.
source: GLASS (Global Antimicrobial Resistance Surveillance System), Lancet Infectious Diseases AMR burden studies
